svg:
Quantifiers: Definitions and Proofs of Theorems
Why Quantifiers Matter Quantifiers express properties about collections: "all students passed" vs "some student failed" They appear in SQL queries, program verification, algorithms, and formal proofs
∀ ∃
Predicates & Propositional Functions P(x): "x is even" | Q(x,y): "x + y = 10" | R(x): "x > 0" Domain: set of all possible values for variables | Example: integers ℤ, real numbers ℝ
Predicate P(x) P(5) = false
Universal Quantifier (∀) ∀x P(x) means: "for ALL x, P(x) is true" True iff P(a) is true for EVERY element a in the domain ∀x (x + 0 = x) "For all integers, adding zero gives the original number"
✓
✓
✓
✓
✓ Every element satisfies P(x)
Existential Quantifier (∃) ∃x P(x) means: "there EXISTS an x such that P(x) is true" True iff there is AT LEAST ONE element a in domain where P(a) holds ∃x (x² = 4) "There exists a number whose square is 4" → x = 2 or x = -2
✗
✗
✓
✗
✗ At least one satisfies P(x)
Theorem 1: Negation of Universal Quantifier ¬∀x P(x) ≡ ∃x ¬P(x) Proof: If not all x satisfy P, then some x exists that doesn't satisfy P
NOT "for all" ⇔ EXISTS one false
¬∀x P(x) ≠ "for all" P(x)
Theorem 2: Negation of Existential Quantifier ¬∃x P(x) ≡ ∀x ¬P(x) Proof: If no x satisfies P, then for all x, P is false
NOT "exists" ⇔ NONE satisfy
¬∃x P(x) = "for none", P(x)
Multiple Quantifiers: Order Matters! ∀x ∀y P(x,y) — for all x and all y | ∃x ∃y P(x,y) — there exist x and y
∀x ∃y (x + y = 0) TRUE: choose y = -x
∃y ∀x (x + y = 0) FALSE: no single y
DIFFERENT!
Bound vs Free Variables
∀x P(x, y) — x is BOUND, y is FREE
y has no truth value!
∃x (x > 0 ∧ x < 10) x is bound
<g class="step" id="step9" data-narration="Example: Negating 'All students passed' uses Theorem 1 to show 'At least one student failed.'"">
Example 1: Negating Universal Statement Problem: "All students in this class passed the exam" Original: ∀x P(x) where P(x) = "x passed"
Negation using Theorem 1: ∃x ¬P(x)
English Translation: "Some student failed"
Example 2: Mathematical Translation Problem: "There is a real number whose square is negative"
∃x (x² < 0)
TRUTH VALUE: FALSE Because ∀x ∈ ℝ, x² ≥ 0
Example 3: Proof with Multiple Quantifiers Prove: ∀x ∈ ℤ ∃y ∈ ℤ (x + y = 0)
Proof: Let x be arbitrary integer. Choose y = -x (which is in ℤ by closure)
Then x + (-x) = 0, so P(x,y) holds. Since x was arbitrary, true for all integers. ∴ TRUE
Summary: Key Formulas
¬∀x P(x) ≡ ∃x ¬P(x)
¬∃x P(x) ≡ ∀x ¬P(x)
∀x ∃y ≠ ∃y ∀x
Common Mistakes to Avoid 1. Forgetting domain | 2. Negating predicate instead of quantifier | 3. Assuming ∀∃ = ∃∀ | 4. Treating free variables as true/false ∀ = "every" | ∃ = "some/exists"
